verses.text: Eliphaz returns more sharply: a wise man would not fill himself with windy talk and unprofitable speech. He charges Job with empty words. Yet to dismiss a sufferer's cry as mere wind is itself a failure of wisdom and love.

He claims Job casts off fear and restrains prayer, so that 'thine own mouth condemneth thee.' He turns Job's laments into evidence of guilt. To twist a man's grief into proof of impiety is a cruel use of words.

Was Job the first man ever born, or privy to God's secret counsel? The aged and grey-headed are on their side. Eliphaz leans on seniority and tradition. But age and numbers do not guarantee that one has rightly read God's ways.

Are the consolations of God too small for Job, that his spirit turns against the Almighty? Eliphaz means his own counsel as God's comfort. The presumption that our words equal God's comforts can make us deaf to a brother's real need.

'What is man, that he should be clean?' he asks; even the heavens are not pure in God's sight, and man drinks iniquity like water. The doctrine of human corruption is true. But Eliphaz uses a true creed only as a club against Job.

He offers to teach Job the wisdom handed down by the fathers, untouched by strangers. He appeals again to ancient tradition. Inherited wisdom has its place, yet it cannot by itself plumb the mystery before them.

The wicked man, he says, writhes in pain all his days, dreadful sounds in his ears, trouble and anguish ready to overwhelm him. He paints the terror of the guilty. The portrait fits some, but he aims it unjustly at the upright Job.

Because such a man stretched out his hand against God and defied the Almighty, he is brought to ruin, dwelling in houses ready to become heaps. Eliphaz pictures the proud rebel's end. The warning is sound, but the target is wrong.

He shall not be rich nor his substance endure, says Eliphaz; let none deceived trust in vanity, for emptiness shall be his reward. He warns against false confidence. To build on what cannot last is indeed to reap nothing but vanity.

Cut off before his time, he is like a vine that sheds its unripe grapes, and the company of the godless is barren. Eliphaz ends with the hypocrite's desolation. True of hypocrites, yet cruelly misapplied to a man whose heart was right with God.
